title: Kinematics of mechanisms
summary: A quick, focused lesson on planar mechanism kinematics: the rigid-body velocity and acceleration fields, the instantaneous-centre method, and relative motion with Coriolis. Then nine fully worked exam problems (rigid body in runners, four-bar, rolling wheels, slider-crank, suspension, cart-in-curve, quick-return, sliding vane, piston-crank) showing the standard velocity-then-acceleration recipe.
objectives: Apply the rigid-body velocity and acceleration fields $\vec v_P=\vec v_O+\vec\omega\times(P-O)$ and $\vec a_P=\vec a_O+\vec\alpha\times(P-O)-\omega^2(P-O)$ to any point of a link
Locate the instantaneous centre of zero velocity by perpendiculars and use it to read off velocities (and recognise it cannot be used for accelerations)
Carry out a full velocity-then-acceleration analysis of a four-bar linkage and a slider-crank by coupling links at pin and slider joints
Handle rolling-without-slip wheels by placing the ICR at the ground contact point
Apply the relative-motion (moving-frame) method, including the Coriolis term $2\vec\omega\times\vec v_{rel}$, to quick-return guides, sliding vanes and radial-groove problems
Identify the centripetal, tangential and Coriolis contributions in an acceleration and combine them correctly by components

blocks.text: What kinematics of mechanisms is about

A planar mechanism is a set of rigid bodies (links) joined so that the whole assembly has one degree of freedom: fix the input motion and **every** velocity and acceleration in the mechanism is determined. The exam problems all reduce to two skills: (1) write the **rigid-body velocity/acceleration field** for each link, and (2) glue links together at the joints (a pin shares position; a slider shares the cross-groove direction but allows sliding). Master those and four-bars, slider-cranks, rolling wheels and quick-returns are all the same recipe.

Rigid-body velocity and acceleration fields. O and P are two points of the SAME rigid body. The acceleration has a tangential part $\vec\alpha\times(P-O)$ and a centripetal part $-\omega^2(P-O)$ pointing from P toward O.

blocks.term: Instantaneous Centre of zero velocity (ICR / point K)
blocks.text: At any instant a planar rigid body rotates about a point K where $\vec v_K=0$. Then for every point P, $\vec v_P=\vec\omega\times(P-K)$, so $v_P=\omega\,\overline{KP}$ and $\vec v_P\perp KP$. K is found as the intersection of the lines drawn **perpendicular to the known velocity directions** of two points. (For a wheel rolling without slipping, K is the ground contact point.)
The ICR is a **velocity-only** trick. Accelerations are NOT zero at K, so never use $a_P=\alpha\cdot KP$ unless K is a truly fixed pivot. For accelerations always go back to the full field formula.

blocks.tex: \vec v_{abs}=\vec v_{drag}+\vec v_{rel}\qquad \vec a_{abs}=\vec a_{drag}+\vec a_{rel}+\underbrace{2\,\vec\omega\times\vec v_{rel}}_{\text{Coriolis}}
blocks.text: Relative-motion (moving-frame) formulas, used whenever a pin slides inside a groove. The drag terms are the velocity/acceleration the point would have if rigidly attached to the moving link; $\vec v_{rel}$, $\vec a_{rel}$ are the sliding along the groove; the Coriolis term $2\vec\omega\times\vec v_{rel}$ appears ONLY in acceleration.

Drag acceleration of a guide point at radius $r$ from a pivot rotating at $\omega,\alpha$: a centripetal part $\omega^2 r$ (toward the pivot) plus a tangential part $\alpha r$ (perpendicular). The Coriolis part $2\omega v_{rel}$ is perpendicular to the groove.

steps.label: 1 Sketch & data
steps.text: Redraw the mechanism in the given configuration. Mark fixed pivots, the input $\omega_1$ (sense!), and all known angles. Convert units (rpm$\to$rad/s: $\omega=\pi n/30$).

steps.label: 2 Velocity of the driven point
steps.text: The input link rotates about a fixed pivot, so $v_A=\omega_1\,\overline{OA}$, direction $\perp OA$.

steps.label: 3 Join the next link
steps.text: For a pin joint use $\vec v_B=\vec v_A+\vec\omega_2\times(B-A)$. Knowing the DIRECTION of $\vec v_B$ (it is $\perp$ to the next rocker, or along the slider line) gives two scalar equations for the two unknowns $\omega_2$ and $v_B$.

steps.label: 3' Sliding joint
steps.text: If a pin slides in a groove, instead use $\vec v_{abs}=\vec v_{drag}+\vec v_{rel}$: project the known absolute velocity onto the groove direction (=$v_{rel}$) and perpendicular to it (=$\omega_{guide}\cdot r$).

steps.label: 4 ICR shortcut (optional)
steps.text: For a single link with two known velocity directions, locate K by perpendiculars and read $\omega=v_A/\overline{KA}$, then any other velocity $v_P=\omega\,\overline{KP}$.

steps.label: 5 Accelerations
steps.text: Repeat with the acceleration field. Each link contributes a centripetal $\omega^2 r$ (known once $\omega$ is found) plus an unknown tangential $\alpha r$. Add the Coriolis term for sliding joints. Project onto two directions to solve for the unknown $\alpha$ and the slider/point acceleration.

blocks.text: Classic mistakes: (1) forgetting the Coriolis term $2\omega v_{rel}$ in problems with a pin sliding in a groove (quick-return, vane, scotch-yoke). (2) Using $a=\alpha\,\overline{KP}$ at the ICR. (3) Dropping the centripetal $-\omega^2(P-O)$ in the acceleration of a coupler. (4) Mixing up the sense of $\omega$, which flips every cross-product sign.

Work with components on x,y axes and the operator $\vec\omega\times\vec r=\omega(-r_y,\,r_x)$ (for $\omega$ out of the page). It is far more reliable than juggling angles, and the two scalar equations from $\vec v_B$ or $\vec a_B$ fall out automatically.

question.prompt: A disc rolls without slipping on the ground; its centre moves at $v_C$. Where is the instantaneous centre of zero velocity?

options.content: At the top of the disc

question.explanation: Rolling without slip means the contact point has zero velocity instantaneously, so it is the ICR. Then $\omega=v_C/r$ and the top point moves at $2v_C$.

question.prompt: In a quick-return mechanism the crank pin slides inside the oscillating guide. Which term MUST be added when computing the pin's absolute acceleration that is absent from the velocity equation?
question.explanation: The Coriolis acceleration $2\vec\omega\times\vec v_{rel}$ exists only because a point slides ($v_{rel}\neq0$) inside a rotating frame ($\omega\neq0$). It has no counterpart in the velocity composition.
